title: Ciabatta with biga
subtitle: High-hydration Italian slipper loaf with an overnight starter
excerpt: Open-crumb ciabatta built on a biga overnight pre-ferment. The high hydration (80%) and minimal handling give the characteristic hole structure. Made in a loaf tin for ease, then turned out.
sourceNotes: Ciabatta was developed in northern Italy in the 1980s as a domestic answer to the baguette; the biga method was popularised by Italian bakers in the Veneto and Lombardy traditions. The Italian Bakers (Maggie Glezer, 2007) and Bread (Jeffrey Hamelman, 2004) document the biga approach used here.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely. Freeze whole or sliced for up to 2 months. Refresh in a hot oven for 5 minutes from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The biga is built the evening before. Once the dough is mixed and rested, it bakes that same morning.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, baking stone or heavy tray preheated
baking.steamMethod: ice cubes on a tray beneath for the first 10 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: An Italian pre-ferment made from flour, a small amount of yeast, and water, left to ferment slowly overnight. The biga develops acidity and flavour compounds that a straight dough cannot build in the same time.

content.text: An 80% hydration dough behaves differently from bread dough of the usual sort. It will not hold a shape in the hand; it spreads. That is the point. The structure comes from the gluten developed in the biga and through careful folding rather than kneading. Work with the dough, not against it.
Wet hands throughout. A dough scraper helps lift the slack dough from the bowl and the surface without tearing the gluten you have built.
Build the biga the night before

ingredients in a bowl until combined. It will be a shaggy, stiff mass. Cover and refrigerate for 10 to 16 hours. By morning it should have roughly doubled, with a web of bubbles visible through the sides.

Tear the cold biga into rough pieces into the bowl. Add the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{whole-milk}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{rapeseed-oil}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}}. Mix with your hand or a stand mixer on medium speed for 8 to 10 minutes until the dough is smooth and extensible. It will still be very slack. Cover and rest for 90 minutes at room temperature.

Perform three sets of stretch-and-folds over the first 45 minutes (every 15 minutes). With wet hands, stretch the dough up from one side and fold it over the centre; rotate 90° and repeat three more times per set. After the third set, leave the dough undisturbed for 15 minutes. Line two baking trays with baking paper and dust with flour. Turn the dough out and divide into two pieces without knocking out air. Transfer to the trays, dust the tops with flour, and leave to proof for 1 hour until puffed and jiggly.

Preheat the oven to 230°C fan with a baking stone or heavy tray inside and a roasting tray on the shelf below. Carefully slide the ciabatta onto the stone. Throw six ice cubes into the lower tray and close the door. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the loaves are deep golden and sound hollow when tapped. Cool on a rack for 30 minutes before tearing.
